43	
44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Lean file
83	<path/to/file.lean>
84	
85	## Blueprint chapter
86	blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex
87	
88	## Known issues
89	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
90	```
91	
92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116	
117	## Report format
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
120	
121	```markdown
122	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Check Report
123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Iteration
128	<NNN>
129	
130	## Files audited
131	- Lean: <path>
132	- Blueprint: <path>
133	
134	## Per-declaration
135	
136	For every `\lean{...}` block in the chapter, one entry:
137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)
141	- **Proof follows sketch**: yes / partial / no / N/A (if N/A: chapter doesn't have a proof body to compare against)
142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143	
144	### `\lean{Foo.baz}` ...
145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.
194	
195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.

Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).

When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \

41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

12	This file is the **A.1.c.SubT** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
13	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It records the dedicated
14	substrate on which the abelian-group instance of the relative Picard quotient
15	`Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) := Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` rests (the residual `sorry`
16	of `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean`).

18	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
19	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
20	`[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three
21	ingredients on the Lean carrier:
22	
23	1. a binary tensor-product operation
24	   `⊗ : Scheme.Modules X × Scheme.Modules X → Scheme.Modules X`;
25	2. the structure sheaf `O_X` as a designated unit object for `⊗`;

32	together at the `Scheme.Modules` level is missing, and there is no
33	`MonoidalCategory` instance on `Scheme.Modules X`. This file records the
34	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) + (3)
35	into a monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X`.

94	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — `restrictScalars` is lax monoidal
95	
96	The presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor along a morphism of
97	presheaves of *commutative* rings is lax monoidal. Mathlib ships the sectionwise
98	fact `ModuleCat.restrictScalars f` is `LaxMonoidal`
99	(`Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction`); here we lift it to
100	the presheaf level through the sectionwise presheaf monoidal structure
101	(`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal`). This is the sole project-side ingredient feeding
102	the oplax comparison `δ` of `pullback φ` (the mate of `pushforward φ`) used to
103	close `tensorObj_restrict_iso`. Per blueprint `lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal`. -/
104	
105	namespace PresheafOfModules
106	
107	universe v'
108	
109	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v'} C] {R S : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat.{u}}
110	
111	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
112	/-- The lax-monoidal unit `ε` of `restrictScalars α`, assembled sectionwise from
113	`ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app X)`'s lax-monoidal unit. -/
114	noncomputable def restrictScalarsLaxε
115	    (α : R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat ⟶ S ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :
116	    (𝟙_ (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _))) ⟶
117	      (restrictScalars α).obj (𝟙_ (PresheafOfModules.{u} (S ⋙ forget₂ _ _))) where
118	  app X := Functor.LaxMonoidal.ε (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app X).hom)
119	  naturality {X Y} f := by
120	    ext r
121	    dsimp
122	    erw [PresheafOfModules.unit_map_one, ModuleCat.restrictScalars_η,
123	      ModuleCat.restrictScalars_η]
124	    simp only [map_one]
125	    erw [PresheafOfModules.unit_map_one]
126	
127	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
128	/-- The lax-monoidal tensorator `μ` of `restrictScalars α`, assembled sectionwise
129	from `ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app X)`'s lax-monoidal tensorator. -/
130	noncomputable def restrictScalarsLaxμ
131	    (α : R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat ⟶ S ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)
132	    (M₁ M₂ : PresheafOfModules.{u} (S ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) :
133	    (restrictScalars α).obj M₁ ⊗ (restrictScalars α).obj M₂ ⟶
134	      (restrictScalars α).obj (M₁ ⊗ M₂) where
135	  app X := by
136	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app X).hom) (M₁.obj X) (M₂.obj X)
137	  naturality {X Y} f := by
138	    refine ModuleCat.MonoidalCategory.tensor_ext (fun m₁ m₂ ↦ ?_)
139	    dsimp
140	    erw [PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_map_tmul, ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul,
141	      ModuleCat.restrictScalars_μ_tmul, PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_map_tmul]
142	    rfl
143	
144	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
145	/-- **`restrictScalars α` is lax monoidal** for a morphism `α` of presheaves of
146	commutative rings. Project-local lift of `ModuleCat.instLaxMonoidalRestrictScalars`. -/
147	noncomputable instance restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal
148	    (α : R ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat ⟶ S ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :
149	    (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars α).LaxMonoidal where
150	  ε := restrictScalarsLaxε α
151	  μ M₁ M₂ := restrictScalarsLaxμ α M₁ M₂
152	  μ_natural_left := by
153	    intro X Y f X'
154	    ext1 Z
155	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ_natural_left (F := ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app Z).hom)
156	      (f.app Z) (X'.obj Z)
157	  μ_natural_right := by
158	    intro X Y X' f
159	    ext1 Z
160	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.μ_natural_right (F := ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app Z).hom)
161	      (X'.obj Z) (f.app Z)
162	  associativity := by
163	    intro M N P
164	    ext1 Z
165	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.associativity (F := ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app Z).hom)
166	      (M.obj Z) (N.obj Z) (P.obj Z)
167	  left_unitality := by
168	    intro M
169	    ext1 Z
170	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.left_unitality (F := ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app Z).hom)
171	      (M.obj Z)
172	  right_unitality := by
173	    intro M
174	    ext1 Z
175	    exact Functor.LaxMonoidal.right_unitality (F := ModuleCat.restrictScalars (α.app Z).hom)
176	      (M.obj Z)

178	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — flat left-whiskering preserves the
179	sheafification localizer
180	
181	The single non-formal ingredient of the `⊗`-invertibility associator
182	(`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso`, blueprint
183	`lem:flat_whisker_localizer`): for a sectionwise-*flat* presheaf of modules `F`
184	and a morphism `g` that is locally injective / locally surjective for the
185	Grothendieck topology `J` (i.e. lies in the sheafification localizer `J.W`), the
186	left-whiskered morphism `F ◁ g` is again locally injective / surjective. Local
187	surjectivity is pure right-exactness of `⊗` (no flatness); local injectivity is
188	where sectionwise flatness enters, via `Module.Flat.lTensor_exact`. All
189	ingredients are present in Mathlib — the route uses **no** `MonoidalClosed`
190	structure. -/
191	
192	section FlatWhisker
193	
194	open MonoidalCategory CategoryTheory.Presheaf
195	
196	variable {J : CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology C}
197	
198	/-- Sectionwise computation: the underlying additive map of `(F ◁ g).app X` is
199	`LinearMap.lTensor (F.obj X) (g.app X).hom`, acting on a simple tensor by
200	`a ⊗ₜ b ↦ a ⊗ₜ g(b)`. -/
201	lemma toPresheaf_whiskerLeft_app_tmul
202	    (F : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _))
203	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)} (g : M ⟶ N) (X : Cᵒᵖ)
204	    (a : F.obj X) (b : M.obj X) :
205	    (((toPresheaf _).map (F ◁ g)).app X).hom (a ⊗ₜ[(R.obj X)] b)
206	      = a ⊗ₜ[(R.obj X)] (g.app X).hom b := by
207	  erw [toPresheaf_map_app_apply]
208	  exact ModuleCat.MonoidalCategory.whiskerLeft_apply _ _ a b
209	
210	/-- The underlying additive map of `(F ◁ g).app X` is `LinearMap.lTensor`. -/
211	lemma toPresheaf_whiskerLeft_app_apply
212	    (F : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _))
213	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)} (g : M ⟶ N) (X : Cᵒᵖ)
214	    (z : (F ⊗ M).obj X) :
215	    (((toPresheaf _).map (F ◁ g)).app X).hom z
216	      = LinearMap.lTensor (R := (R.obj X : CommRingCat)) (F.obj X) (g.app X).hom z := by
217	  erw [toPresheaf_map_app_apply, PresheafOfModules.whiskerLeft_app]
218	
219	/-- **Local surjectivity is preserved by left-whiskering.** Right-exactness of
220	`⊗`: no flatness needed. Blueprint `lem:flat_whisker_localizer`, surjectivity
221	half. -/
222	lemma isLocallySurjective_whiskerLeft
223	    (F : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _))
224	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)} (g : M ⟶ N)
225	    (hg : IsLocallySurjective J g) :
226	    IsLocallySurjective J (F ◁ g) := by
227	  constructor
228	  intro U s
229	  induction s using TensorProduct.induction_on with
230	  | zero =>
231	      refine J.superset_covering ?_ (J.top_mem U)
232	      intro V i _
233	      exact ⟨0, by rw [map_zero]; exact (map_zero _).symm⟩
234	  | tmul a b =>
235	      refine J.superset_covering ?_ (hg.imageSieve_mem b)
236	      intro V i hi
237	      obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ := hi
238	      refine ⟨(F.map i.op).hom a ⊗ₜ[(R.obj (Opposite.op V))] c, ?_⟩
239	      rw [toPresheaf_whiskerLeft_app_tmul]
240	      erw [presheaf_map_apply_coe, PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_map_tmul]
241	      congr 1
242	  | add s t hs ht =>
243	      refine J.superset_covering ?_ (J.intersection_covering hs ht)
244	      intro V i hi
245	      obtain ⟨⟨ds, hds⟩, ⟨dt, hdt⟩⟩ := hi
246	      refine ⟨ds + dt, ?_⟩
247	      rw [map_add, hds, hdt]; exact (map_add _ s t).symm
248	
249	/-- **Local injectivity is preserved by flat left-whiskering.** This is where
250	sectionwise flatness of `F` enters: via `Module.Flat.lTensor_exact` on the
251	kernel exact sequence `ker(gₓ) ↪ M(X) →gₓ N(X)`, an element of `ker(F ◁ g)` is
252	a sum of simple tensors with kernel entries, each of which restricts to `0` on a
253	covering sieve (local injectivity of `g`). Blueprint `lem:flat_whisker_localizer`,
254	injectivity half. -/
255	lemma isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_flat
256	    (F : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _))
257	    [∀ X, Module.Flat (R.obj X) (F.obj X)]
258	    {M N : PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)} (g : M ⟶ N)
259	    (hg : IsLocallyInjective J g) :
260	    IsLocallyInjective J (F ◁ g) := by
261	  constructor
262	  intro X ξ η h
263	  -- View the sectionwise map of `g` as `R.obj X`-linear (the ring is commutative).
264	  let gl : ((M.obj X : ModuleCat _) : Type _) →ₗ[(R.obj X : CommRingCat)]
265	      ((N.obj X : ModuleCat _) : Type _) := (g.app X).hom
266	  -- `h` says `F ◁ g` agrees on `ξ, η`, i.e. `lTensor` kills `ξ - η`.
267	  have hδ : LinearMap.lTensor (R := (R.obj X : CommRingCat)) (F.obj X) gl (ξ - η) = 0 := by
268	    have heq : LinearMap.lTensor (R := (R.obj X : CommRingCat)) (F.obj X) gl ξ
269	        = LinearMap.lTensor (R := (R.obj X : CommRingCat)) (F.obj X) gl η := by
270	      rw [← toPresheaf_whiskerLeft_app_apply, ← toPresheaf_whiskerLeft_app_apply]; exact h
271	    exact (map_sub (LinearMap.lTensor (R := (R.obj X : CommRingCat)) (F.obj X) gl) ξ η).trans
272	      (sub_eq_zero.mpr heq)
273	  -- Flatness: `ker(F ⊗ gl) = range(F ⊗ ker.subtype)`, so `ξ - η` is a sum of simple
274	  -- tensors with kernel entries.
275	  have hex : Function.Exact
276	      (LinearMap.lTensor (R := (R.obj X : CommRingCat)) (F.obj X) (LinearMap.ker gl).subtype)
277	      (LinearMap.lTensor (R := (R.obj X : CommRingCat)) (F.obj X) gl) :=
278	    Module.Flat.lTensor_exact (F.obj X) (LinearMap.exact_subtype_ker_map gl)
279	  obtain ⟨ζ, hζ⟩ := (hex (ξ - η)).mp hδ
280	  -- Each simple tensor `a ⊗ k` with `gl k = 0` restricts to `0` on a covering sieve
281	  -- (local injectivity of `g`); induct on the witness `ζ`.
282	  have key : ∀ ζ : TensorProduct (R.obj X) (F.obj X) (LinearMap.ker gl),
283	      Presheaf.equalizerSieve (F := (toPresheaf _).obj (F ⊗ M)) (X := X)
284	        (LinearMap.lTensor (R := (R.obj X : CommRingCat)) (F.obj X)
285	          (LinearMap.ker gl).subtype ζ) 0 ∈ J X.unop := by
286	    intro ζ
287	    induction ζ using TensorProduct.induction_on with
288	    | zero =>
289	        rw [map_zero]
290	        exact J.superset_covering (Presheaf.equalizerSieve_self_eq_top _).ge (J.top_mem _)
291	    | tmul a kk =>
292	        rw [LinearMap.lTensor_tmul]
293	        have hk : ((toPresheaf _).map g).app X kk.1
294	            = ((toPresheaf _).map g).app X (0 : ((toPresheaf _).obj M).obj X) := by
295	          rw [map_zero]
296	          erw [toPresheaf_map_app_apply]
297	          exact kk.2
298	        refine J.superset_covering ?_ (hg.equalizerSieve_mem kk.1 0 hk)
299	        intro V f hf
300	        rw [Presheaf.equalizerSieve_apply] at hf ⊢
301	        rw [map_zero] at hf ⊢
302	        erw [presheaf_map_apply_coe, PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_map_tmul]
303	        erw [presheaf_map_apply_coe] at hf
304	        rw [Submodule.subtype_apply, hf]
305	        erw [TensorProduct.tmul_zero]; rfl
306	    | add ζ₁ ζ₂ h₁ h₂ =>
307	        rw [map_add]

354	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
355	
356	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
357	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
358	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
359	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
360	
361	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
362	body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body lifts
363	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
364	the small Zariski site of `X`. -/
365	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
366	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
367	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
368	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
369	
370	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
371	
372	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
373	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
374	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
375	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
376	
377	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. iter-202 Lane TS
378	scaffold: the body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body inherits the
379	morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
380	sheafification. -/
381	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
382	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
383	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
384	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
385	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) f.val g.val)
386	
387	/-- **`⊗`-invertibility of an `𝒪_X`-module.** (Blueprint
388	`def:scheme_modules_isinvertible`.) `M : X.Modules` is `⊗`-invertible when it
389	admits a tensor inverse: an object `N` with `M ⊗_X N ≅ 𝒪_X`, where
390	`𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the designated unit. This is the
391	scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's `Module.Invertible`; the predicate carries its
392	inverse witness existentially, so the dual needed by the relative Picard group
393	law is definitional. -/
394	def IsInvertible {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : Prop :=
395	  ∃ N : X.Modules, Nonempty (tensorObj M N ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)

515	factor `W.ι = (X.homOfLE hWU) ≫ U.ι`, transport through `restrictFunctorCongr`

517	`(M.restrict U.ι).restrict (X.homOfLE hWU)`, restrict the given trivialisation
518	`e` along that open immersion, and identify the restricted unit with the unit
519	via `restrictFunctorIsoPullback` + `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` (an isomorphism
520	because the open immersion's `Opens.map` is `Final`). -/
521	noncomputable def restrictIsoUnitOfLE {X : Scheme.{u}} {M : X.Modules} {U W : X.Opens}
522	    (hWU : W ≤ U)
523	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
524	    M.restrict W.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (W : Scheme).ringCatSheaf := by
525	  have hWU' : W ≤ (𝟙 X) ⁻¹ᵁ U := hWU
526	  set j : (W : Scheme) ⟶ (U : Scheme) := Scheme.Hom.resLE (𝟙 X) U W hWU' with hj
527	  have hjι : j ≫ U.ι = W.ι := by rw [hj, Scheme.Hom.resLE_comp_ι, Category.comp_id]
528	  haveI : (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map j.base).Final :=
529	    CategoryTheory.final_of_representablyFlat _
530	  -- M.restrict W.ι ≅ (pullback W.ι).obj M
531	  refine (Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback W.ι).app M ≪≫ ?_
532	  -- ≅ (pullback (j ≫ U.ι)).obj M
533	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr hjι.symm).app M ≪≫ ?_
534	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj ((pullback U.ι).obj M)
535	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp j U.ι).symm.app M ≪≫ ?_
536	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj (M.restrict U.ι)
537	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullback j).mapIso
538	    ((Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback U.ι).symm.app M) ≪≫ ?_
539	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj 𝒪_U
540	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullback j).mapIso e ≪≫ ?_
541	  -- ≅ 𝒪_W
542	  haveI hI : IsIso (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit j.toRingCatSheafHom) := inferInstance

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the structure-sheaf unit are present as
89	scheme-level objects. Rather than the geometric local-triviality route, the
90	group law is built on \(\otimes\)-\emph{invertibility}: an object is a line
91	object when it admits a tensor inverse, \(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong
92	\mathcal{O}_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), exactly Mathlib's
93	\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom. Under this predicate requirement (3)
94	becomes the three monoidal \emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) ---
95	associator, unitors and braiding --- each obtained cheaply: the unitors and
96	braiding as \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the corresponding
97	presheaf-level isomorphism, and the associator (only needed on the invertible
98	--- hence flat --- objects the law multiplies) as a three-step composite that
99	absorbs the two nested sheafifications by applying the sheafification functor to
100	the unit morphism whiskered by a flat object, with the presheaf-level associator
101	transported in between, while the inverse is carried by the predicate itself.
102	These
103	assemble the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units group is
104	the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The geometric
105	compatibility isomorphism \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\)
106	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and any separate tensor-inverse lemma are
107	\emph{off} this critical path. The full symmetric monoidal category on
108	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is also \emph{not} required; see
109	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
110	
111	\section{Mathlib API survey}
112	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
113	
114	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
115	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
116	
117	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
118	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
119	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
120	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
121	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
122	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
123	sectionwise tensor product
124	\[
125	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
126	\]
127	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
128	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
129	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
130	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
131	
132	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
133	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
134	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
135	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
136	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
137	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
138	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
139	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
140	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
141	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
142	tensor-product operation
143	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
144	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
145	
146	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
147	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
148	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
149	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
150	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
151	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The data this operation must supply for the group law are the monoidal
152	\emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) --- the associator
153	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the left/right unitors
154	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) and the braiding
155	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The unitors and braiding are obtained by the
156	cheap pattern \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) applied to the corresponding
157	presheaf-level isomorphism on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\); the associator,
158	restricted to the \(\otimes\)-invertible --- hence flat --- objects the group
159	law actually multiplies, is obtained as the three-step composite of
160	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: apply the sheafification functor to the unit
161	morphism whiskered by a flat object to absorb each of the two nested
162	sheafifications (once on each side), with the presheaf-level associator
163	transported by \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) in between. The only non-formal
164	input is the flat-whiskering bridge \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}: whiskering
165	a sheafification-localizer morphism by a flat object stays in the localizer
166	class, which holds objectwise by flatness (local injectivity from
167	\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\), local
168	surjectivity from right-exactness of \(\otimes\)). Neither route uses the
169	abstract \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) adjoint, a
170	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure on presheaves of modules, or a bundled
171	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) instance on the localizer class.
172	Together with the
173	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
174	(which carries the inverse existentially) these assemble the commutative monoid
175	of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units group
176	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The project does \emph{not} require a
177	global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on all quasi-coherent
178	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure:
179	monoidal coherence beyond these three isomorphisms is never consumed by a group
180	law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The
181	open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
182	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is \emph{not} on this critical path and is
183	retained only as an optional supplement.
184	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
185	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
186	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
187	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
188	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
189	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
190	independently of any upstream timing.
191	
192	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
193	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
194	
195	\begin{definition}
196	
197	\leanok
198	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
199	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
200	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
201	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
202	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
203	  \[
204	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
205	  \]
206	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
207	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
208	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
209	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
210	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
211	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
212	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
213	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
214	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
215	  canonical compatibility
216	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
217	     \;\cong\;
218	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
219	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
220	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
221	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
222	
223	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
224	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
225	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
226	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
227	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
228	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
229	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
230	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
231	\end{definition}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}
234	
235	\leanok
236	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
237	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
238	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
239	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
240	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
241	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
242	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
243	  \[
244	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
245	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
246	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
247	  \]
248	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
249	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
250	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
251	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
252	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
253	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
254	  \[
255	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
256	    \qquad
257	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
258	  \]
259	  the associator
260	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
261	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
262	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
263	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
264	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
265	\end{lemma}
266	
267	\begin{proof}
268	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
269	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
270	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
271	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
272	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
273	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
274	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
275	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
276	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
277	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
278	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
279	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
280	\end{proof}
281	
282	\begin{remark}
283	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
284	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
285	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
286	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
287	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
288	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
289	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
290	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
291	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
292	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
293	  depend on it.
294	
295	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
296	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
297	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
298	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
299	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
300	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
301	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
302	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
303	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
304	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
305	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
306	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
307	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
308	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
309	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
310	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
311	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
312	
313	  Crucially, the group law restricts that stability to \emph{flat} whiskering
314	  objects, where it becomes elementary and needs no closed structure: for
315	  \emph{flat} \(F\) the implication \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) is
316	  exactly the bridge \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}, proved objectwise from
317	  flatness (local injectivity via
318	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\), local
319	  surjectivity via right-exactness of \(\otimes\)). Since every
320	  \(\otimes\)-invertible module is flat, this flat-scoped stability is all the
321	  group law's associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}) consumes, and it
322	  sidesteps the \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) wall that the arbitrary-\(F\) version
323	  would hit.
324	
325	  The group law needs only the coherence isomorphisms
326	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}:
327	  the unitors and braiding by \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the
328	  presheaf-level isomorphisms, and the associator by the flat-whiskering
329	  three-step composite above. None of these is built from the off-path
330	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
331	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
332	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
333	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
334	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
335	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
336	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
337	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
338	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
339	\end{remark}

341	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
342	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
343	
344	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
345	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
346	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
347	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
348	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
349	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the restriction-compatibility
350	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
351	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
352	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
353	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
354	cover, together with their restriction to the
355	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
356	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
357	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
358	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
359	
360	\begin{lemma}
361	
362	\leanok
363	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
364	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
365	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
366	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
367	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
368	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
369	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
370	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
371	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
372	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
373	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
374	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
375	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
376	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
377	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
378	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
379	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
380	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
381	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
382	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
383	  \[
384	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
385	      \;\;:=\;\;
386	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
387	  \]
388	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
389	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
390	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
391	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
392	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
393	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
394	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
395	\end{lemma}
396	
397	\begin{proof}
398	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
399	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
400	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
401	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
402	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
403	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
404	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
405	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
406	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
407	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
408	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
409	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
410	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
411	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
412	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
413	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
414	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
415	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
416	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
417	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
418	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
419	
420	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
421	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
422	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
423	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
424	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
425	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
426	\end{proof}

428	\begin{lemma}
429	
430	\leanok
431	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
432	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
433	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
434	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
435	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
436	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
437	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
438	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
439	  morphism
440	  \[
441	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
442	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
443	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
444	  \]
445	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
446	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
447	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
448	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
449	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
450	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
451	\end{lemma}
452	
453	\begin{proof}
454	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
455	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
456	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
457	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
458	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
459	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
460	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
461	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
462	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
463	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
464	  isomorphism}
465	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
466	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
467	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
468	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
469	  therefore extension of scalars along
470	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
471	
472	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
473	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
474	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
475	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
476	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
477	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
478	  the abstract pullback functor.
479	
480	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
481	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
482	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
483	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
484	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
485	   \;\cong\;
486	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
487	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
488	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
489	  comparison
490	  \[
491	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
492	      \;\longrightarrow\;
493	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
494	      \;\otimes\;
495	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
496	  \]
497	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
498	
499	  \emph{Step 3 (off the critical path).} Completing this comparison at the
500	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor
501	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
502	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the
503	  pinned commit. The genuine residual decomposes into two Mathlib-absent
504	  presheaf-level ingredients --- a presheaf-level identification
505	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) via
506	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (needing a presheaf-level pushforward
507	  adjunction, only the sheaf version of which exists), and a strong-monoidal
508	  upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the ring isomorphism
509	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). This lemma is therefore \emph{not} on the critical path
510	  for the relative Picard group law: the group law is built from
511	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) and the
512	  coherence isomorphisms
513	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
514	  none of which consume restriction-compatibility. The statement is retained
515	  only as an optional supplement; its full proof is deferred.
516	\end{proof}

518	\begin{lemma}
519	
520	\leanok
521	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
522	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
523	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
524	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
525	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
526	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
527	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
528	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
529	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
530	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
531	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
532	\end{lemma}
533	
534	\begin{proof}
535	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
536	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
537	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
538	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
539	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
540	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
541	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
542	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).

544	
545	\begin{lemma}
546	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
547	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
548	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat}
549	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
550	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
551	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
552	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
553	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
554	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
555	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
556	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
557	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
558	  left-whiskered morphism
559	  \[
560	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
561	  \]
562	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
563	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
564	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
565	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
566	\end{lemma}
567	
568	\begin{proof}
569	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
570	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
571	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
572	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
573	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
574	
575	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
576	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
577	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
578	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
579	  \[
580	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
581	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
582	  \]
583	
584	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
585	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
586	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
587	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
588	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
589	
590	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
591	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
592	  linear maps (Mathlib
593	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
594	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
595	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
596	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
597	
598	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
599	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
600	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
601	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
602	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
603	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
604	\end{proof}
605	
606	\begin{lemma}
607	
608	\leanok
609	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible objects]
610	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
611	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
612	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
613	        lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
614	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
615	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
616	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), i.e.\ locally free of rank one.
617	  There is an isomorphism
618	  \[
619	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
620	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
621	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
622	  \]
623	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). This is an \emph{objectwise}
624	  existence-of-isomorphism statement for the three fixed invertible objects
625	  \(M, N, P\) --- not a natural associator on a category --- and that is
626	  exactly the datum consumed by the group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
627	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), where associativity is the
628	  proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong
629	  M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\).
630	\end{lemma}
631	
632	\begin{proof}
633	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
634	        lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
635	  The associator is built directly from the presheaf-of-modules monoidal data
636	  by absorbing the two nested sheafifications, with the only non-formal input
637	  being the flat-whiskering bridge \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
638	
639	  \emph{Notation.} Write \(a = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) for the
640	  sheafification functor and \(M^{\flat}\) for the underlying presheaf of an
641	  object \(M\), so that the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is
642	  \(M \otimes_X N = a\bigl(M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}\bigr)\), with
643	  \(\otimes^{p}\) the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
644	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}). Let
645	  \(\eta = \mathtt{toSheafify}\) be the sheafification unit, the canonical
646	  morphism \(A \to a(A)^{\flat}\); it lies in the sheafification localizer class
647	  \(J.W\), being locally injective and locally surjective
648	  (\(\mathtt{instIsLocallyInjectiveToSheafify}\)). Let \(\alpha\) be the
649	  associator of the presheaf-of-modules monoidal category,
650	  \[
651	    \alpha \;\colon\;
652	      (M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}) \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}
653	        \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
654	      M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} (N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}).
655	  \]
656	
657	  \emph{Flatness is free.} Since \(M, N, P\) are \(\otimes\)-invertible they are
658	  projective, hence flat (Mathlib
659	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible} \Rightarrow \mathtt{Projective} \Rightarrow
660	  \mathtt{Flat}\), via \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.of\_projective}\)). The flatness
661	  hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} is therefore satisfied by each
662	  of \(M, N, P\) at no cost.
663	
664	  \emph{The nested form.} Unfolding the substrate on both sides exposes an inner
665	  sheafification:
666	  \[
667	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
668	      \;=\; a\Bigl(\, a\bigl(M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat}
669	              \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\Bigr),
670	    \qquad
671	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
672	      \;=\; a\Bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p}
673	              a\bigl(N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat} \,\Bigr).
674	  \]
675	  The required isomorphism is the three-step composite:
676	  \begin{enumerate}
677	    \item \emph{Absorb the inner sheafification on the left.} The unit
678	      \(\eta_{M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}}\) lies in \(J.W\); its
679	      right-whiskering by the flat object \(P\),
680	      \(\eta_{M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}} \triangleright P^{\flat}\),
681	      again lies in \(J.W\) by \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (\(P\) flat).
682	      Applying the sheafification functor \(a\) inverts it:
683	      \[
684	        a\Bigl(\, a\bigl(M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat}
685	              \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\Bigr)
686	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
687	        a\bigl(\, (M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}) \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\bigr).
688	      \]
689	    \item \emph{Transport the presheaf associator.} Apply \(a\) to \(\alpha\):
690	      \[
691	        a\bigl(\, (M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}) \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\bigr)
692	          \;\xrightarrow[\ \sim\ ]{\;a.\mathtt{mapIso}\,\alpha\;}\;
693	        a\bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} (N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}) \,\bigr).
694	      \]
695	    \item \emph{Restore the inner sheafification on the right.} The unit
696	      \(\eta_{N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}}\) lies in \(J.W\); its
697	      left-whiskering by the flat object \(M\),
698	      \(M^{\flat} \triangleleft \eta_{N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}}\), again
699	      lies in \(J.W\) by \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (\(M\) flat). Applying
700	      \(a\) inverts it:
701	      \[
702	        a\bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} (N^{\flat} \otimes^{p} P^{\flat}) \,\bigr)
703	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
704	        a\Bigl(\, M^{\flat} \otimes^{p}

1106	  coherence isomorphisms, so \(M \otimes_X N\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible.
1107	  Well-definedness of \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\) on iso-classes is the
1108	  bifunctoriality of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}. The
1109	  group axioms are propositions about iso-classes and are discharged by the
1110	  coherence isomorphisms, each evaluated on the invertible objects in play:
1111	  associativity by \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (whose hypotheses are exactly
1112	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility of the three arguments), the unit laws by
1113	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity by
1114	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}; no pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
1115	  invoked, since each axiom asserts only the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism.
1116	  Every class \([M]\) has an inverse \([N]\) with
1117	  \([M \otimes_X N] = [\mathcal{O}_X]\), namely the tensor inverse carried by
1118	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}).
1119	  Therefore the iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible modules form an abelian
1120	  group --- the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- equivalently the
1121	  group of units of the (un-built) commutative monoid of all iso-classes. The
1122	  whole assembly follows the Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\)
1123	  template.
1124	\end{proof}
